title: Index Theory and Flexibility in Positive Scalar Curve Geometry
speaker: Bernhard Hanke

proof_stages.label: Spin Dirac obstruction in the compact case
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls spin structures, the half-spinor splitting, the Dirac operator, and the Lichnerowicz-Weitzenbock formula, then combines the spectral gap under uniformly positive scalar curvature with the Atiyah-Singer index theorem to obtain the classical vanishing of the A-hat genus obstruction.

proof_stages.label: Noncompact positive-scalar-curvature problem and coarse viewpoint
proof_stages.summary: The noncompact question for T^{n-2} x R^2 motivates replacing Fredholm index theory by Roe's large-scale/coarse index theory, which is designed to handle complete noncompact manifolds and codimension-two obstructions.

proof_stages.label: Roe coarse index construction and computability
proof_stages.summary: For a complete spin manifold, the self-adjoint closure of the Dirac operator, functional calculus, the C_0(M)-module structure, local compactness, and finite propagation produce a coarse index in the K-theory of the Roe algebra, with computable cases and quasi-isometry-invariant applications such as enlargeability.

proof_stages.label: Partitioned-manifold contradiction and codimension-two theorem
proof_stages.summary: Assuming uniformly positive scalar curvature on the torus-plane product, one removes a tubular neighborhood and doubles to get a partitioned manifold with positive scalar curvature outside a compact set, so Roe's partial vanishing theorem contradicts nonvanishing detected by the partitioned-manifold index theorem; the same mechanism yields a general spin codimension-two obstruction.

proof_stages.label: Low-regularity curvature-flexibility theorem
proof_stages.summary: The second part states that any C^2 Riemannian metric can be C^1-approximated by a C^{1,1} metric whose curvature satisfies prescribed inequalities on an open dense subset, and explains why neither C^2 regularity nor an everywhere curvature bound can generally be required.

proof_stages.label: Partial differential relations and local flexibility lemma
proof_stages.summary: Curvature bounds are encoded as open relations in jet bundles, and Gromov's local flexibility lemma is stated and sketched via a cutoff in the deformation parameter whose derivatives are controlled so that an initially local deformation extends globally while remaining in the relation.

proof_stages.label: Local implantation of high curvature
proof_stages.summary: In normal coordinates around a point, the given metric is interpolated with a constant-curvature model so that the one-jet is fixed at the point, and the local flexibility lemma spreads this deformation to the whole manifold while creating a small ball of high curvature and only an arbitrarily small global curvature loss.

proof_stages.label: Dense iteration and weak C^{1,1} limit
proof_stages.summary: The local construction is iterated over a countable dense set with summable curvature losses, after which weak compactness and a diagonal argument give a C^{1,1} limit metric lying in the prescribed C^1-neighborhood and satisfying the curvature bound on the dense union of implanted neighborhoods.

examples.name: The manifold T^{n-2} x R^2

examples.engagement_reason: The speaker runs the coarse-index obstruction on T^{n-2} x R^2 by removing a tubular neighborhood, doubling, using a partitioned-manifold index theorem, and deriving a contradiction.

examples.name: Compact spaces in the Roe algebra
examples.note: The compact-manifold case is used as a sanity check showing that local compactness forces the Roe algebra to be the compact operators and hence recovers the classical Fredholm index.
examples.engagement_reason: For compact M, the speaker checks that locally compact Roe operators are just compact operators and identifies the resulting K-theory index with the usual Fredholm index.

examples.name: High-genus surface and Gauss-Bonnet puzzle
examples.note: A compact surface of positive genus illustrates the subtlety that a C^{1,1} metric can have curvature at least one on an open dense subset while the Gauss-Bonnet integral remains negative.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker sets up the high-genus surface case, writes the limiting Gauss curvature integral, invokes Gauss-Bonnet, and analyzes the apparent contradiction.

passes.reason: a high-genus surface is structurally ordinary but its negative Euler characteristic is used via Gauss-Bonnet as an obstruction to everywhere positive curvature
A high-genus surface with curvature positive on an open dense set exposes the failure of the naive Gauss-Bonnet positivity inference.
